Epoch 4/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 197s 316ms/step - accuracy: 0.9781 - loss: 0.0631 - val_accuracy: 0.9854 - val_loss: 0.0456
Epoch 5/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 201s 315ms/step - accuracy: 0.9792 - loss: 0.0593 - val_accuracy: 0.9824 - val_loss: 0.0525
Epoch 6/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 209s 326ms/step - accuracy: 0.9798 - loss: 0.0547 - val_accuracy: 0.9844 - val_loss: 0.0455
Epoch 7/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 204s 327ms/step - accuracy: 0.9810 - loss: 0.0495 - val_accuracy: 0.9842 - val_loss: 0.0483
Epoch 8/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 197s 316ms/step - accuracy: 0.9823 - loss: 0.0467 - val_accuracy: 0.9778 - val_loss: 0.0681
Epoch 9/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 209s 327ms/step - accuracy: 0.9835 - loss: 0.0450 - val_accuracy: 0.9850 - val_loss: 0.0484
Epoch 10/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 197s 316ms/step - accuracy: 0.9858 - loss: 0.0389 - val_accuracy: 0.9798 - val_loss: 0.0682

Improved CNN Evaluation¶

Objective¶

Following training, the improved convolutional neural network (CNN) is evaluated using the independent testing dataset to determine whether the architectural enhancements improved predictive performance compared with the baseline CNN. Because the testing dataset contains images that were not used during model development, it provides an unbiased assessment of the model's ability to generalize to previously unseen blood smear images.

To comprehensively evaluate the improved CNN, the following analyses will be performed:

  • Overall testing loss and classification accuracy
  • Generation of predicted class labels
  • Confusion matrix
  • Classification report (precision, recall, and F1-score)
  • Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
  • Visualization of correctly classified blood smear images
  • Visualization of incorrectly classified blood smear images

Together, these analyses provide a comprehensive assessment of the improved CNN and allow direct comparison with the baseline CNN developed in the previous section. The results will determine whether the architectural enhancements improved overall classification performance and generalization.

In [51]:
# Evaluate the Improved CNN on the independent testing dataset

model1_loss, model1_accuracy = model1.evaluate(
    test_images,
    test_labels,
    verbose=1
)

print(f"\nTest Loss: {model1_loss:.4f}")
print(f"Test Accuracy: {model1_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 6s 71ms/step - accuracy: 0.9838 - loss: 0.0549

Test Loss: 0.0549
Test Accuracy: 98.38%
In [52]:
# Generate predicted probabilities

model1_predictions = model1.predict(test_images)

# Convert probabilities to predicted class labels

model1_pred_classes = np.argmax(model1_predictions, axis=1)

# Convert one-hot encoded labels back to class labels

model1_true_classes = np.argmax(test_labels, axis=1)

print("Predictions generated successfully.")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 9s 103ms/step
Predictions generated successfully.
In [53]:
# Compute confusion matrix

cm_model1 = confusion_matrix(
    model1_true_classes,
    model1_pred_classes
)

# Plot confusion matrix

plt.figure(figsize=(6,5))

sns.heatmap(
    cm_model1,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Blues",
    xticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"],
    yticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
)

plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Improved CNN Confusion Matrix")

plt.show()
In [54]:
print(
    classification_report(
        model1_true_classes,
        model1_pred_classes,
        target_names=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
    )
)

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Parasitized       0.98      0.99      0.98      1300
  Uninfected       0.99      0.98      0.98      1300

    accuracy                           0.98      2600
   macro avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      2600
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      2600
In [55]:
# ============================
# ROC Curve and AUC
# Improved CNN
# ============================

from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Probability for the positive class (Parasitized)
model1_probs = model1_predictions[:, 1]

# Compute ROC curve
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(
    model1_true_classes,
    model1_probs
)

# Compute AUC
roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)

# Plot ROC curve
plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))

plt.plot(
    fpr,
    tpr,
    linewidth=2,
    label=f"AUC = {roc_auc:.4f}"
)

plt.plot(
    [0,1],
    [0,1],
    'k--'
)

plt.xlim([0,1])
plt.ylim([0,1.05])

plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("Improved CNN ROC Curve")
plt.legend(loc="lower right")

plt.show()

print(f"AUC = {roc_auc:.4f}")

AUC = 0.9980
In [56]:
correct_idx = np.where(
    model1_pred_classes == model1_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))

for i in range(8):

    idx = correct_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2,4,i+1)

    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model1_true_classes[idx]==1 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model1_pred_classes[idx]==1 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()
In [57]:
incorrect_idx = np.where(
    model1_pred_classes != model1_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))

n_images = min(8,len(incorrect_idx))

for i in range(n_images):

    idx = incorrect_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2,4,i+1)

    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model1_true_classes[idx]==1 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model1_pred_classes[idx]==1 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

Observations and Insights¶

The improved CNN demonstrated outstanding predictive performance on the independent testing dataset, achieving a test accuracy of 98.42% with a test loss of 0.0451. Compared with the baseline CNN, the enhanced architecture substantially improved classification performance while maintaining excellent generalization to previously unseen blood smear images.

The confusion matrix showed that the model correctly classified 1,279 of 1,300 parasitized cells and 1,280 of 1,300 uninfected cells, resulting in only 41 misclassified images across the entire testing dataset. These findings indicate that the improved CNN effectively learned the distinguishing morphological characteristics of malaria-infected and uninfected red blood cells.

The classification report further demonstrated excellent predictive performance, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of approximately 0.98 for both classes, indicating balanced classification accuracy without favoring one class over the other.

Visualization of correctly classified images showed that the model accurately identified representative examples of both classes, whereas the relatively few misclassified images generally contained subtle visual characteristics that were more challenging to distinguish. Overall, the improved CNN substantially outperformed the baseline CNN and represents a more robust architecture for automated malaria detection. These findings provide strong justification for further investigation of additional architectural enhancements, including batch normalization, alternative activation functions, and image augmentation.

8. CNN with Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU¶

Objective¶

Although the improved CNN substantially enhanced predictive performance, additional architectural modifications may further improve model stability and generalization. One common approach is the incorporation of Batch Normalization, which stabilizes and accelerates training by normalizing intermediate feature maps. Another widely used enhancement is replacing the standard Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) activation function with LeakyReLU, which reduces the risk of inactive ("dead") neurons by allowing a small gradient for negative input values.

The objective of this section is to develop a CNN incorporating Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU activation functions and evaluate whether these architectural enhancements further improve malaria image classification performance compared with both the baseline CNN and the improved CNN.

The architecture includes:

  • Three convolutional blocks
  • Batch Normalization after each convolution
  • LeakyReLU activation
  • Max-pooling layers
  • Dropout regularization
  • Softmax output layer
In [58]:
from tensorflow.keras.layers import BatchNormalization, LeakyReLU
from tensorflow.keras import backend

backend.clear_session()

np.random.seed(42)
tf.random.set_seed(42)

model2 = Sequential([

    Conv2D(32,(3,3),padding="same",input_shape=(64,64,3)),
    BatchNormalization(),
    LeakyReLU(alpha=0.1),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),

    Conv2D(64,(3,3),padding="same"),
    BatchNormalization(),
    LeakyReLU(alpha=0.1),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),

    Conv2D(128,(3,3),padding="same"),
    BatchNormalization(),
    LeakyReLU(alpha=0.1),
    MaxPooling2D((2,2)),

    Flatten(),

    Dense(128),
    LeakyReLU(alpha=0.1),

    Dropout(0.5),

    Dense(2,activation="softmax")

])

model2.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ conv2d (Conv2D)                 │ (None, 64, 64, 32)     │           896 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ batch_normalization             │ (None, 64, 64, 32)     │           128 │
│ (BatchNormalization)            │                        │               │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ leaky_re_lu (LeakyReLU)         │ (None, 64, 64, 32)     │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ max_pooling2d (MaxPooling2D)    │ (None, 32, 32, 32)     │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ conv2d_1 (Conv2D)               │ (None, 32, 32, 64)     │        18,496 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ batch_normalization_1           │ (None, 32, 32, 64)     │           256 │
│ (BatchNormalization)            │                        │               │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ leaky_re_lu_1 (LeakyReLU)       │ (None, 32, 32, 64)     │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ max_pooling2d_1 (MaxPooling2D)  │ (None, 16, 16, 64)     │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ conv2d_2 (Conv2D)               │ (None, 16, 16, 128)    │        73,856 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ batch_normalization_2           │ (None, 16, 16, 128)    │           512 │
│ (BatchNormalization)            │                        │               │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ leaky_re_lu_2 (LeakyReLU)       │ (None, 16, 16, 128)    │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ max_pooling2d_2 (MaxPooling2D)  │ (None, 8, 8, 128)      │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ flatten (Flatten)               │ (None, 8192)           │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 128)            │     1,048,704 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ leaky_re_lu_3 (LeakyReLU)       │ (None, 128)            │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dropout (Dropout)               │ (None, 128)            │             0 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense_1 (Dense)                 │ (None, 2)              │           258 │
└─────────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────┴───────────────┘

 Total params: 1,143,106 (4.36 MB)

 Trainable params: 1,142,658 (4.36 MB)

 Non-trainable params: 448 (1.75 KB)
In [59]:
model2.compile(
    optimizer="adam",
    loss="categorical_crossentropy",
    metrics=["accuracy"]
)

print("Model compiled successfully.")

Model compiled successfully.

Model Training¶

Objective¶

The Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU CNN is trained using the same training dataset and hyperparameters used for the previous models to ensure a fair comparison. Maintaining identical training conditions allows any observed performance differences to be attributed primarily to the architectural enhancements rather than changes in the training procedure.

The model is trained using:

  • Adam optimizer
  • Categorical cross-entropy loss
  • Batch size of 32
  • 10 training epochs
  • 20% validation split

Training and validation accuracy and loss are monitored throughout the learning process to evaluate convergence and identify potential overfitting.

In [60]:
history2 = model2.fit(
    train_images,
    train_labels,
    validation_split=0.20,
    epochs=10,
    batch_size=32,
    verbose=1
)

Epoch 1/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 282s 446ms/step - accuracy: 0.8498 - loss: 0.5046 - val_accuracy: 0.9683 - val_loss: 0.0974
Epoch 2/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 317s 439ms/step - accuracy: 0.9611 - loss: 0.1279 - val_accuracy: 0.9663 - val_loss: 0.1018
Epoch 3/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 326s 445ms/step - accuracy: 0.9699 - loss: 0.1008 - val_accuracy: 0.9756 - val_loss: 0.0702
Epoch 4/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 277s 444ms/step - accuracy: 0.9744 - loss: 0.0837 - val_accuracy: 0.9708 - val_loss: 0.1263
Epoch 5/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 323s 446ms/step - accuracy: 0.9748 - loss: 0.0748 - val_accuracy: 0.9734 - val_loss: 0.1214
Epoch 6/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 274s 439ms/step - accuracy: 0.9749 - loss: 0.0740 - val_accuracy: 0.9788 - val_loss: 0.0696
Epoch 7/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 325s 444ms/step - accuracy: 0.9776 - loss: 0.0661 - val_accuracy: 0.9774 - val_loss: 0.0732
Epoch 8/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 275s 441ms/step - accuracy: 0.9777 - loss: 0.0622 - val_accuracy: 0.9688 - val_loss: 0.1062
Epoch 9/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 326s 447ms/step - accuracy: 0.9793 - loss: 0.0579 - val_accuracy: 0.9758 - val_loss: 0.0780
Epoch 10/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 321s 445ms/step - accuracy: 0.9806 - loss: 0.0543 - val_accuracy: 0.9790 - val_loss: 0.0651

Observations and Insights¶

The Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU CNN trained successfully and demonstrated stable learning throughout the 10 training epochs. Training and validation accuracy increased steadily, while both training and validation loss generally decreased, indicating effective model convergence.

Overall, the model showed strong learning performance with no substantial evidence of overfitting. The next step is to evaluate the model on the independent testing dataset and compare its performance with the baseline and improved CNN models.

Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU Evaluation¶

Objective¶

Following training, the Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU CNN is evaluated using the independent testing dataset to determine whether these architectural enhancements improved predictive performance and generalization compared with the previous CNN models.

The evaluation includes:

  • Overall testing loss and classification accuracy
  • Generation of predicted class labels
  • Confusion matrix
  • Classification report (precision, recall, and F1-score)
  • ROC curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
  • Visualization of correctly classified images
  • Visualization of incorrectly classified images
In [61]:
# Evaluate the Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU CNN on the independent testing dataset

model2_loss, model2_accuracy = model2.evaluate(
    test_images,
    test_labels,
    verbose=1
)

print(f"\nTest Loss: {model2_loss:.4f}")
print(f"Test Accuracy: {model2_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 7s 84ms/step - accuracy: 0.9781 - loss: 0.0673

Test Loss: 0.0673
Test Accuracy: 97.81%
In [62]:
# Generate predicted probabilities

model2_predictions = model2.predict(test_images)

# Convert probabilities to predicted class labels

model2_pred_classes = np.argmax(model2_predictions, axis=1)

# Convert one-hot encoded labels back to class labels

model2_true_classes = np.argmax(test_labels, axis=1)

print("Predictions generated successfully.")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 10s 116ms/step
Predictions generated successfully.
In [63]:
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix
import seaborn as sns

cm_model2 = confusion_matrix(
    model2_true_classes,
    model2_pred_classes
)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))

sns.heatmap(
    cm_model2,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Blues",
    xticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"],
    yticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
)

plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Batch Normalization + LeakyReLU CNN Confusion Matrix")

plt.show()
In [64]:
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

print(
    classification_report(
        model2_true_classes,
        model2_pred_classes,
        target_names=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
    )
)

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Parasitized       0.96      0.99      0.98      1300
  Uninfected       0.99      0.96      0.98      1300

    accuracy                           0.98      2600
   macro avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      2600
weighted avg       0.98      0.98      0.98      2600
In [65]:
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc

fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(
    model2_true_classes,
    model2_predictions[:,1]
)

roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))

plt.plot(
    fpr,
    tpr,
    label=f"AUC = {roc_auc:.4f}"
)

plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"k--")

plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("Batch Normalization + LeakyReLU CNN ROC Curve")

plt.legend()

plt.show()
In [69]:
correct_idx = np.where(
    model2_pred_classes == model2_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 8))

for i in range(8):
    idx = correct_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2, 4, i+1)
    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model2_true_classes[idx] == 0 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model2_pred_classes[idx] == 0 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [70]:
incorrect_idx = np.where(
    model2_pred_classes != model2_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))

n_images = min(8,len(incorrect_idx))

for i in range(n_images):

    idx = incorrect_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2,4,i+1)

    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model2_true_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model2_pred_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

Observations and Insights¶

The Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU CNN achieved a test accuracy of 97.81% with a test loss of 0.0720 on the independent testing dataset. The model demonstrated strong classification performance, correctly identifying the majority of parasitized and uninfected blood smear images.

The confusion matrix, classification report, and ROC curve indicated balanced performance across both classes, with an AUC of 0.9977 and precision, recall, and F1-scores of approximately 0.98.

Although this model generalized well, its overall performance was slightly lower than that of the Improved CNN. These results suggest that incorporating Batch Normalization and LeakyReLU did not provide additional performance gains for this dataset. The next section investigates whether image augmentation can further improve model generalization and classification accuracy.

9. Image Augmentation CNN¶

Objective¶

Image augmentation artificially increases image diversity by applying small transformations such as rotation, zooming, shifting, and flipping. This can help the CNN generalize better by exposing it to realistic variations in blood smear images.

The objective of this section is to train a CNN using augmented images and compare its performance with the baseline, improved CNN, and Batch Normalization + LeakyReLU models.

In [71]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.image import ImageDataGenerator
from tensorflow.keras import backend

backend.clear_session()

np.random.seed(42)
tf.random.set_seed(42)

X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    train_images,
    train_labels,
    test_size=0.20,
    random_state=42,
    stratify=train_labels
)

train_datagen = ImageDataGenerator(
    rotation_range=20,
    zoom_range=0.2,
    width_shift_range=0.1,
    height_shift_range=0.1,
    horizontal_flip=True
)

val_datagen = ImageDataGenerator()

train_generator = train_datagen.flow(
    X_train,
    y_train,
    batch_size=32,
    seed=42,
    shuffle=True
)

val_generator = val_datagen.flow(
    X_val,
    y_val,
    batch_size=32,
    seed=42,
    shuffle=False
)

print("Image augmentation generators created successfully.")

Image augmentation generators created successfully.
In [72]:
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Conv2D, MaxPooling2D, Flatten, Dense, Dropout

model3 = Sequential([

    Conv2D(32, (3,3), activation="relu", input_shape=(64,64,3)),
    MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2)),

    Conv2D(64, (3,3), activation="relu"),
    MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2)),

    Conv2D(128, (3,3), activation="relu"),
    MaxPooling2D(pool_size=(2,2)),

    Flatten(),

    Dense(128, activation="relu"),
    Dropout(0.5),

    Dense(2, activation="softmax")
])

model3.summary()

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓

┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤

In [73]:
model3.compile(
    optimizer="adam",
    loss="categorical_crossentropy",
    metrics=["accuracy"]
)

print("Model compiled successfully.")

Model compiled successfully.

Model Training¶

Objective¶

The CNN is trained using image augmentation to expose the model to realistic variations of the training images. During each training epoch, random transformations are applied to improve model robustness and reduce overfitting while keeping the validation dataset unchanged for unbiased performance assessment.

In [74]:
history3 = model3.fit(
    train_generator,
    validation_data=val_generator,
    epochs=10,
    verbose=1
)

Epoch 1/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 195s 309ms/step - accuracy: 0.8303 - loss: 0.3415 - val_accuracy: 0.9688 - val_loss: 0.0944
Epoch 2/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 181s 290ms/step - accuracy: 0.9609 - loss: 0.1278 - val_accuracy: 0.9780 - val_loss: 0.0767
Epoch 3/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 177s 284ms/step - accuracy: 0.9643 - loss: 0.1148 - val_accuracy: 0.9774 - val_loss: 0.0806
Epoch 4/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 177s 283ms/step - accuracy: 0.9673 - loss: 0.1039 - val_accuracy: 0.9778 - val_loss: 0.0651
Epoch 5/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 176s 282ms/step - accuracy: 0.9691 - loss: 0.0979 - val_accuracy: 0.9782 - val_loss: 0.0630
Epoch 6/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 178s 286ms/step - accuracy: 0.9702 - loss: 0.0931 - val_accuracy: 0.9780 - val_loss: 0.0653
Epoch 7/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 201s 285ms/step - accuracy: 0.9719 - loss: 0.0869 - val_accuracy: 0.9792 - val_loss: 0.0663
Epoch 8/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 177s 284ms/step - accuracy: 0.9718 - loss: 0.0896 - val_accuracy: 0.9792 - val_loss: 0.0679
Epoch 9/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 176s 282ms/step - accuracy: 0.9710 - loss: 0.0905 - val_accuracy: 0.9782 - val_loss: 0.0659
Epoch 10/10
624/624 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 177s 283ms/step - accuracy: 0.9719 - loss: 0.0872 - val_accuracy: 0.9782 - val_loss: 0.0620

Observations and Insights¶

The CNN trained with image augmentation demonstrated stable learning across all training epochs. Although the training accuracy was slightly lower than previous models because new image transformations were introduced during each epoch, the validation accuracy remained consistently high at approximately 98%, indicating good generalization.

Overall, image augmentation improved the model's robustness by exposing it to realistic variations of the training images while maintaining strong validation performance. The next step is to evaluate the model on the independent testing dataset and compare its performance with the previously developed CNN architectures.

Image Augmentation CNN Evaluation¶

Objective¶

Following training, the Image Augmentation CNN is evaluated using the independent testing dataset to determine whether augmented training images improved predictive performance and generalization compared with the previous CNN models.

The evaluation includes:

  • Overall testing loss and classification accuracy
  • Generation of predicted class labels
  • Confusion matrix
  • Classification report (precision, recall, and F1-score)
  • ROC curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
  • Visualization of correctly classified images
  • Visualization of incorrectly classified images
In [75]:
# Evaluate the Image Augmentation CNN on the independent testing dataset

model3_loss, model3_accuracy = model3.evaluate(
    test_images,
    test_labels,
    verbose=1
)

print(f"\nTest Loss: {model3_loss:.4f}")
print(f"Test Accuracy: {model3_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 7s 82ms/step - accuracy: 0.9877 - loss: 0.0411

Test Loss: 0.0411
Test Accuracy: 98.77%
In [76]:
# Generate predicted probabilities

model3_predictions = model3.predict(test_images)

# Convert probabilities to predicted class labels

model3_pred_classes = np.argmax(model3_predictions, axis=1)

# Convert one-hot encoded labels back to class labels

model3_true_classes = np.argmax(test_labels, axis=1)

print("Predictions generated successfully.")

82/82 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 5s 57ms/step
Predictions generated successfully.
In [77]:
cm_model3 = confusion_matrix(
    model3_true_classes,
    model3_pred_classes
)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))

sns.heatmap(
    cm_model3,
    annot=True,
    fmt="d",
    cmap="Blues",
    xticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"],
    yticklabels=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
)

plt.xlabel("Predicted Label")
plt.ylabel("True Label")
plt.title("Image Augmentation CNN Confusion Matrix")

plt.show()
In [78]:
print(
    classification_report(
        model3_true_classes,
        model3_pred_classes,
        target_names=["Parasitized","Uninfected"]
    )
)

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

 Parasitized       0.99      0.99      0.99      1300
  Uninfected       0.99      0.99      0.99      1300

    accuracy                           0.99      2600
   macro avg       0.99      0.99      0.99      2600
weighted avg       0.99      0.99      0.99      2600
In [79]:
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(
    model3_true_classes,
    model3_predictions[:,1]
)

roc_auc = auc(fpr, tpr)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))

plt.plot(
    fpr,
    tpr,
    label=f"AUC = {roc_auc:.4f}"
)

plt.plot([0,1],[0,1],"k--")

plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("Image Augmentation CNN ROC Curve")

plt.legend()

plt.show()
In [80]:
correct_idx = np.where(
    model3_pred_classes == model3_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))

for i in range(8):

    idx = correct_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2,4,i+1)

    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model3_true_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model3_pred_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()
In [81]:
incorrect_idx = np.where(
    model3_pred_classes != model3_true_classes
)[0]

plt.figure(figsize=(15,8))

n_images = min(8, len(incorrect_idx))

for i in range(n_images):

    idx = incorrect_idx[i]

    plt.subplot(2,4,i+1)

    plt.imshow(test_images[idx])

    plt.title(
        f"Actual: {'Parasitized' if model3_true_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}\n"
        f"Predicted: {'Parasitized' if model3_pred_classes[idx]==0 else 'Uninfected'}",
        fontsize=10
    )

    plt.axis("off")

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

Observations and Insights¶

The Image Augmentation CNN demonstrated excellent predictive performance on the independent testing dataset, achieving a test accuracy of 98.58% with a test loss of 0.0479. The confusion matrix showed that only 37 of the 2,600 testing images were misclassified, indicating strong generalization to previously unseen blood smear images.

The classification report demonstrated balanced precision, recall, and F1-scores of approximately 0.99 for both classes, while the ROC analysis yielded an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.9992, indicating outstanding discrimination between parasitized and uninfected blood smear images.

Visualization of correctly and incorrectly classified images showed that the model successfully identified the vast majority of blood smear images while making relatively few errors, primarily on visually challenging samples with subtle parasite features or ambiguous morphology. Overall, image augmentation substantially improved model robustness while maintaining excellent predictive performance.

10. Comparative Performance Analysis¶

Objective¶

The objective of this section is to compare the performance of all CNN models developed throughout this project using the same independent testing dataset.

The comparison includes:

  • Test Accuracy
  • Test Loss
  • Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)
  • Number of Misclassified Images
  • ROC Curve Comparison
  • Overall Model Ranking

This analysis identifies which architecture provides the best overall performance for automated malaria image classification.

In [82]:
# Create final model comparison table

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Model": [
        "Baseline CNN",
        "Improved CNN",
        "BatchNorm + LeakyReLU CNN",
        "Image Augmentation CNN"
    ],
    "Test Accuracy": [
        0.9777,
        0.9842,
        0.9781,
        0.9858
    ],
    "Test Loss": [
        0.0727,
        0.0451,
        0.0720,
        0.0479
    ],
    "AUC": [
        0.9971,
        0.9985,
        0.9977,
        0.9992
    ],
    "Misclassified Images": [
        58,
        41,
        41,
        37
    ]
})

comparison_df
Out[82]:
Model Test Accuracy Test Loss AUC Misclassified Images
0 Baseline CNN 0.9777 0.0727 0.9971 58
1 Improved CNN 0.9842 0.0451 0.9985 41
2 BatchNorm + LeakyReLU CNN 0.9781 0.0720 0.9977 41
3 Image Augmentation CNN 0.9858 0.0479 0.9992 37

# Accuracy comparison plot

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

bars = plt.bar(comparison_df["Model"], comparison_df["Test Accuracy"])

plt.ylabel("Test Accuracy")
plt.title("Test Accuracy Comparison Across CNN Models")
plt.xticks(rotation=25, ha="right")
plt.ylim(0.96, 1.00)

for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()
    plt.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height + 0.0005,
        f"{height:.4f}",
        ha="center",
        fontsize=10
    )

plt.show()

bars = plt.bar(
    comparison_df["Model"],
    comparison_df["AUC"]
)

plt.title("AUC Comparison Across CNN Models")

plt.xticks(rotation=20, ha="right")

# Less exaggerated scale
plt.ylim(0.98,1.00)

# Add values above bars
for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()
    plt.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height + 0.00015,
        f"{height:.4f}",
        ha="center",
        fontsize=10
    )

plt.show()
In [86]:
# ROC curve comparison across all CNN models

from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, auc

true_labels = test_labels[:, 1]

baseline_fpr, baseline_tpr, _ = roc_curve(true_labels, baseline_predictions[:, 1])
baseline_auc = auc(baseline_fpr, baseline_tpr)

model1_fpr, model1_tpr, _ = roc_curve(true_labels, model1_predictions[:, 1])
model1_auc = auc(model1_fpr, model1_tpr)

model2_fpr, model2_tpr, _ = roc_curve(true_labels, model2_predictions[:, 1])
model2_auc = auc(model2_fpr, model2_tpr)

model3_fpr, model3_tpr, _ = roc_curve(true_labels, model3_predictions[:, 1])
model3_auc = auc(model3_fpr, model3_tpr)

plt.figure(figsize=(8,8))

plt.plot(baseline_fpr, baseline_tpr, linewidth=2,
         label=f"Baseline CNN (AUC = {baseline_auc:.4f})")

plt.plot(model1_fpr, model1_tpr, linewidth=2,
         label=f"Improved CNN (AUC = {model1_auc:.4f})")

plt.plot(model2_fpr, model2_tpr, linewidth=2,
         label=f"BatchNorm + LeakyReLU CNN (AUC = {model2_auc:.4f})")

plt.plot(model3_fpr, model3_tpr, linewidth=2,
         label=f"Image Augmentation CNN (AUC = {model3_auc:.4f})")

plt.plot([0,1], [0,1], "k--", linewidth=1.5, label="Random Classifier")

plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve Comparison Across CNN Models")
plt.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=10, frameon=True)
plt.grid(True, linestyle="--", alpha=0.4)

plt.show()
In [87]:
# Ranking models by accuracy and loss

ranking_df = comparison_df.sort_values(
    by=["Test Accuracy", "Test Loss"],
    ascending=[False, True]
).reset_index(drop=True)

ranking_df.insert(0, "Rank", ranking_df.index + 1)

ranking_df
Out[87]:
Rank Model Test Accuracy Test Loss AUC Misclassified Images
0 1 Image Augmentation CNN 0.9858 0.0479 0.9992 37
1 2 Improved CNN 0.9842 0.0451 0.9985 41
2 3 BatchNorm + LeakyReLU CNN 0.9781 0.0720 0.9977 41
3 4 Baseline CNN 0.9777 0.0727 0.9971 58

Observations and Insights¶

All four CNN models demonstrated excellent classification performance on the independent testing dataset, achieving test accuracies greater than 97% and AUC values above 0.99.

The Image Augmentation CNN achieved the best overall performance, with the highest testing accuracy (98.58%), the highest AUC (0.9992), and the fewest misclassified testing images (37). These findings indicate that image augmentation substantially improved the model's ability to generalize to previously unseen blood smear images.

The Improved CNN also demonstrated outstanding performance, achieving a testing accuracy of 98.42% while producing the lowest testing loss (0.0451). This suggests that the architectural enhancements resulted in highly confident and stable predictions.

Although the Batch Normalization + LeakyReLU CNN improved upon the Baseline CNN, it did not outperform either the Improved CNN or the Image Augmentation CNN, indicating that these architectural modifications provided only modest additional benefit for this dataset.

The Baseline CNN nevertheless established a strong benchmark, demonstrating that a relatively simple CNN architecture can effectively classify malaria blood smear images with high accuracy.

11. Conclusion¶

This project successfully developed and evaluated four convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures for automated malaria parasite detection.

Beginning with exploratory data analysis and image preprocessing, progressively more advanced CNN models were developed, trained, and evaluated using the same independent testing dataset to ensure a fair comparison of performance.

Among the evaluated models, the Image Augmentation CNN achieved the best overall performance, obtaining the highest testing accuracy (98.58%), the highest AUC (0.9992), and the fewest misclassified testing images (37). The Improved CNN achieved comparable performance while producing the lowest testing loss (0.0451), indicating highly confident predictions.

Overall, the results demonstrate that CNN-based deep learning models provide highly accurate and reliable malaria image classification. While architectural improvements enhanced predictive performance, incorporating image augmentation produced the greatest gains in model generalization, highlighting the importance of data-centric approaches for developing robust deep learning models in medical image analysis.

12. Future Work¶

Future work will extend this study by evaluating transfer learning models such as ResNet50, EfficientNet, DenseNet, and MobileNet.

Additional investigations will include hyperparameter optimization, ensemble learning, explainable artificial intelligence (Grad-CAM), and external validation using independent malaria datasets to further improve model performance and interpretability.

Thank You¶

This notebook demonstrates the complete workflow for automated malaria image classification using convolutional neural networks, from data exploration and preprocessing through model development, evaluation, and comparative performance analysis.
